elements.sent-2411-001: Compositional zero-shot learning (CZSL) aims to recognize novel compositions of attributes and objects learned from seen compositions.
elements.sent-2411-002: Previous works disentangle attribute and object by extracting shared and exclusive parts between image pairs sharing the same attribute (object), as well as aligning them with pretrained word embeddings to improve unseen attribute-object recognition.
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite the significant achievements of existing efforts, they are hampered by three limitations: (1) the efficacy of disentanglement is compromised due to the influence of the background and the intricate entanglement of attribute with object in the same parts.
elements.sent-2411-004: Being aware of these, we propose a novel framework named Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) embeddings and atTRibute smoothIng guiDEd diseNTanglement (TRIDENT) for CZSL.
elements.sent-2411-005: First, we leverage feature adaptive aggregation modules to mitigate the impact of background, and utilize learnable condition masks to capture multigranularity features for disentanglement.
elements.sent-2411-006: Then, the last hidden states of MLLM are employed as word embeddings for their superior representation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, we propose attribute smoothing with auxiliary attributes generated by Large Language Model (LLM) for seen compositions, addressing the issue of overconfidence by encouraging the model to learn more attributes in one given composition.
elements.sent-2411-008: Extensive experiments demonstrate that TRIDENT achieves state-of-the-art performance on three benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-009: As for the study of compositional generalization ability inherent to humans, compositional zero-shot learning (CZSL) \citep{2017_Misra_CVPR_first, 2018_Nagarajan_ECCV_AttrAsOp, 2019_Purushwalkam_ICCV_TMN is proposed to enable machines to recognize unseen attribute-object compositions by leveraging knowledge of attributes and objects (\ie, primitives) learned from seen compositions.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Specifically, in the training phase, models are provided with images and compositional labels (\eg, ripe orange and peeled apple).
elements.sent-2411-011: During the testing phase, given an image depicting a novel composition (\eg , peeled orange), models are assigned to classify the image into the corresponding category \citep{2022_Zhang_ECCV_INV .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Prior works \citep{2017_Misra_CVPR_first, 2019_Nan_AAAI focus on mapping the visual features and the word embeddings of compositions into a joint space.}
elements.sent-2411-013: These methods have poor generalization capability to unseen compositions, as they fail to learn primitives.
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, recent studies \citep{2022_Saini_CVPR_OADis, 2023_Hao_CVPR_ADE, 2024_Li_TCDS consider visual disentanglement.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Among them, some prominent works deploy a triplet of images to disentangle: a given image (noted as the main image), and two supplementary images, each sharing either the same attribute or the same object as the main image.
elements.sent-2411-016: The triplet of images is treated as two image pairs for subsequent analysis.
elements.sent-2411-017: These approaches aim to disentangle attribute and object by analyzing the shared and exclusive features of the image pair, as well as aligning them with word embeddings (\eg, GloVe \citep{2014_Pen_EMNLP_glove ), as shown in Figure \ref{fig:vice-pipline .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Although these pioneer research studies have achieved great progress, they exhibit three limitations: L1: Disentanglement is impeded due to the influence of the background and the intricate entanglement of attribute with object in the same parts of image.
elements.sent-2411-019: On the one hand, models tend to extract the background feature unique to one image in the pair as the disentangled exclusive features.
elements.sent-2411-020: On the other hand, some existing methods \citep{2021_Ruis_NIPS_ProtoProp, 2022_Saini_CVPR_OADis compute the similarity of image pairs for disentanglement at the spatial level.}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, disentangling attribute and object at the spatial level presents significant challenges because they entangle in the same spatial features.
elements.sent-2411-022: Taking an image of ripe apple as an example, the spatial regions corresponding to the attribute "ripe" and the object "apple" are fully co-located.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{images/vice-pipline.pdf} \caption{A general comparison between the existing method and our proposed TRIDENT. Note that, we only present the representation learning of an image pair sharing the object for brevity.} \label{fig:vice-pipline}
elements.sent-2411-023: L2: Existing word embeddings lack the depth needed to capture complex multimodal semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-024: To begin with, word embeddings, such as Word2Vec \citep{2013_mikolov_arxiv_word2vec and GloVe \citep{2014_Pen_EMNLP_glove , are grounded in word frequency and contextual co-occurrence, rather than capturing high-level semantic nuances\citep{2021_sarzynska_detecting .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, the process of aligning visual features with word embeddings can be viewed as a form of cross-modal matching; however, these word embeddings are trained only in a single text modal, failing to capture cross-modal information between images and texts.
elements.sent-2411-026: L3: Existing methods display excessive confidence in seen compositions, impairing their ability to generalize toward novel compositions.
elements.sent-2411-027: Due to the one-hot label used during training, these approaches are limited by learning only one attribute and object, neglecting the fact that objects naturally exhibit multiple attributes \citep{2024_xu_arxiv_mac .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Consequently, models exhibit overconfidence in the disentangled ground-truth attribute, treating other attributes that can describe the object as negative attributes, which results in the diminished performance on unseen compositions.
elements.sent-2411-029: To address the aforementioned limitations, we propose a novel framework named Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) embeddings and atTRibute smoothIng guiDEd diseNTanglement (TRIDENT), which consists of three major modules: visual feature extraction, attribute-object disentanglement, and feature alignment.
elements.sent-2411-030: The first module leverages feature adaptive aggregation (FAA) modules to mitigate the impact of background noise, and exploits learnable condition masks to learn multi-granularity features to improve subsequent disentanglement.
elements.sent-2411-031: The second module aims at leveraging shared and exclusive weights of image pairs to disentangle attribute and object under the the paradigm that apart from the shared features of the image pair, each image has its own exclusive features.
elements.sent-2411-032: The third module is intended to align the visual features of compositions and disentangled primitives with the last hidden states of an MLLM, LLaVA v1.5 \citep{2024_liu_CVPR_llava1.5 , \ie, MLLM embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-033: This is inspired by some works \citep{2020_wang_ASLP_SBERT, 2022_Mu_arxiv_sgpt, 2024_muennighoff_GritLM, 2023——formage , which find that the last hidden states of (M)LLM exhibit powerful representational capabilities in embedding tasks, such as retrieval and classification.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, to tackle the issue that the ineffective overconfidence of the models regarding ground-truth attribute hinders them from generalizing to unseen compositions, we exploit a Large Language Model (LLM), GPT-3.5 \citep{2023_openai_gpt35 to generate auxiliary attributes based on attribute-object compositions and perform label smoothing for attributes, \ie, attribute smoothing.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In summary, the contributions of our work are three-fold: 1.
elements.sent-2411-036: We propose novel feature adaptive aggregation modules to reduce the impact of background, and utilize learnable condition masks to capture multi-granularity features for disentanglement in CZSL.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-037: We employ both LLM and MLLM to guide attribute-object disentanglement by generating auxiliary attributes and representing word embeddings, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-038: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to leverage both LLM and MLLM to advance disentanglement in CZSL task.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-039: We conduct extensive experiments to evaluate our method on three CZSL benchmarks, showing that TRIDENT has achieved state-of-the-art performance.
elements.sent-2411-040: The source code will be released soon \footnote{\url{https://github.com/xud-yan/Trident .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Prior works in CZSL can be broadly divided into two main streams.
elements.sent-2411-043: One main stream is to learn representations of compositions in a joint space.
elements.sent-2411-044: SymNet \citep{2020_Li_CVPR_SymNet proposes to learn symmetry property in compositions.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Co-CGE \citep{2022_Mancini_PAMI_CoCGE leverages a Graph Convolutional Neural Network to learn compositional representations.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The other main stream aims at disentangling visual representations of primitives to reduce composition learning into primitive learning.
elements.sent-2411-047: SCEN \citep{Li_2022_CVPR_SCEN leverages contrastive loss to excavate discriminative prototypes of primitives.}
elements.sent-2411-048: OADis \citep{2022_Saini_CVPR_OADis disentangles primitives by affinity modules.}
elements.sent-2411-049: CANet \citep{2023_Wang_CVPR_CANET learns conditional attribute conditioned on the recognized object and the input image.}
elements.sent-2411-050: More recent works \citep{2023_Saini_ICLR_CSP, 2023_Lu_CVPR_DFSP, 2024_Huang_CVPR_Troika focus on leveraging the encyclopedic knowledge of pretrained vision-language models (VLM), such as Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) \citep{2021_Radford_ICML_CLIP and Context Optimization (CoOp) \citep{Zhou_2022_CVPR_Coop , to encode and align images and texts.}}}

elements.sent-2411-053: Early models, such as BERT \citep{2019_devlinACL_bert and GPT-2 \citep{2019_Radford_Arxiv_GPT2 , initially exhibit strong capabilities in understanding and generating human-like language.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Subsequently, GPT-3 \citep{2020_Brown_NIPS_GPT3 and LLaMA \citep{2023_touvron_arxiv_llama demonstrate great breakthroughs across numerous language benchmarks.}}

elements.sent-2411-057: Flamingo \citep{2022_NIPS_Flamingo first integrates Vision Transformer (ViT) \citep{2020_Dosovitskiy_Vit and LLM by gated cross-attention.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Recently, LLaVA \citep{2024_liu_NIPS_llava and LLaVA v1.5 \citep{2024_liu_CVPR_llava1.5 introduce visual instruction tuning to enhance instruction following capability.}}

elements.sent-2411-060: CMC processes visual features before the last layer of ViT, aligning the visual space of ViT with the linguistic space of LLM.
elements.sent-2411-061: We choose LLaVA v1.5 as our foundational MLLM as it has demonstrated state-of-the-art performance across various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-062: Recently, exploring the powerful language capabilities of (M)LLM to handle representation tasks (\eg, retrieval) has emerged as a prominent research domain.
elements.sent-2411-063: SGPT \citep{2022_Mu_arxiv_sgpt exploits the last hidden states of LLM for the input token sequence or a special learnable token to derive representational embeddings.}

elements.sent-2411-132: The loss for disentangled attributes can be defined as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{attr} = \sum_{0.65{$c \in \{m2a, a2m, m2o, o2m\}$}}{} H_{ls}(F_{attr}^{e}, \mathcal{Y}) + H_{oh}(F_{attr}^{ma}, \mathcal{Y}) \end{aligned}\end{equation} Concerning the disentangled object features, we use cross-entropy with one-hot label to learn the prototype of the object and the loss is as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{obj} = \sum_{0.7{$c \in \{m2a, a2m, m2o, o2m\}$}} H_{oh}(F_{obj}^{e}, \mathcal{Y}) + H_{oh}(F_{obj}^{mo}, \mathcal{Y}) \end{aligned}\end{equation} With respect to the visual feature of the image from image embedder, we calculate the cosine similarity between visual embedding and the composed pair embedding of the corresponding composition label and use one-hot label to align them.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation} L_{attr} = \sum_{0.65{$c \in \{m2a, a2m, m2o, o2m\}$}}{} H_{ls}(F_{attr}^{e}, Y) + H_{oh}(F_{attr}^{ma}, Y) \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation} L_{obj} = \sum_{0.7{$c \in \{m2a, a2m, m2o, o2m\}$}} H_{oh}(F_{obj}^{e}, Y) + H_{oh}(F_{obj}^{mo}, Y) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-133: The classification loss for compositions is as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{comp} = \sum_{img \in \{m, a, o\}} H_{oh}(F_{comp}^{img}, \mathcal{C}_s)\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-134: During the training phase, the overall loss function is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \gamma_{ortho} \mathcal{L}_{ortho} + \gamma_{comp} \mathcal{L}_{comp} + \gamma_{attr} \mathcal{L}_{attr} + \gamma_{obj} \mathcal{L}_{obj}\end{equation} where $\gamma_{ortho}$, $\gamma_{comp}$, $\gamma_{attr}$, and $\gamma_{obj}$ are weighting factors to balance the influence of different losses.
elements.sent-2411-135: For inference, we use the composition feature space generated by the classifier that is obtained by optimizing $\mathcal{L _{comp}$.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Specifically, given an image from test set, the cosine similarities of its visual feature obtained by image embedder and the composed pair embeddings of all candidate compositions in the test set are computed.
elements.sent-2411-137: The composition with the highest similarity is the class predicted by the model.

elements.section-2411-007: Obtainment of The Last Hidden States of MLLM
elements.sent-2411-225: We input the attribute (object) word into LLaVA v1.5, which first tokenizes the word into z tokens.
elements.sent-2411-226: These tokens pass through all attention blocks in the MLLM, ultimately generating z embeddings of dimension $d_{m}$ after the last block, named the last hidden states.
elements.sent-2411-227: Subsequently, we apply average pooling to these z embeddings of dimension $d_{m}$ to obtain a $d_m$-dimensional embedding that represents the attribute.
elements.sent-2411-228: Since the last hidden states are designed to generate the next token rather than for representation, \citet{2024_muennighoff_GritLM leverages instruction to fine-tune the model.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Therefore, we fine-tune the last hidden states with a low learning rate during the training phase of TRIDENT.
elements.sent-2411-230: It is important to note that although LLaVA v1.5 may have seen certain images during training, the model is asked to generate textual descriptions of images in an autoregressive manner during training.
elements.sent-2411-231: The textual descriptions focus on the main content of the image, rather than the "attribute-object" label.
elements.sent-2411-232: Therefore, there is no issue of data leakage when training TRIDENT.
elements.sent-2411-233: MIT-states consists of diverse real-world images labeled by early image search engine technology.

elements.sent-2411-492: To provide more insight into the effect of visual features and label smoothing, we study the performance of TRIDENT with respect to different numbers of visual features and different label smoothing factors, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-493: Experiments are conducted on two datasets MIT-States and C-GQA.
elements.sent-2411-494: In Figure~\ref{fig:p-mit , the performance of our model on MIT-States generally improves with the increasing number of visual features, but subsequently declines.}
elements.sent-2411-495: This trend is reasonable, as a greater number of visual features contain more useful information, thereby enhancing the performance.
elements.sent-2411-496: However, in Figure~\ref{fig:p-cgqa , as the number of visual features increases, the performance of the model on C-GQA tends to decline overall.}
elements.sent-2411-497: Increasing the number of features, however, results in heightened model complexity without tangible benefits, potentially impairing the performance of the model.
elements.sent-2411-498: In contrast, the MIT-States dataset exhibits significant noise; thus, while the increase in the number of visual features may introduce more noise, it also necessitates a greater amount of useful information, which can effectively mitigate the impact of the noise.

elements.section-2411-015: Do Auxiliary Attributes Help or Hurt?
elements.sent-2411-503: Ablation study in the full paper proves attribute smoothing is indeed helpful.
elements.sent-2411-504: In addition, MIT-States has 115 attributes, and the set of auxiliary attributes for the dataset has 621 elements.
elements.sent-2411-505: According to statistics, 543 auxiliary attributes (the majority) do not appear in the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-506: Smoothing with these auxiliary attributes does not mislead the model into mistakenly classifying images into other attributes in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-507: In addition, changing the smoothing factor $\alpha$ and the number of auxiliary attributes $t$ can control the degree of smoothness to achieve the optimal performance.
